AFM Expands Air Wing With New Aircraft And Helicopter In €50 Million Boost
Malta’s Armed Forces are stepping things up in the air.
The Armed Forces of Malta has announced plans to expand its Air Wing with a new King Air aircraft and an AW139 helicopter, as demand for operations continues to rise.
The new additions, part of a €50 million investment, are expected to arrive next year.
According to the AFM, activity within its Air Division has increased significantly over the past six years, with aircraft and helicopter operations rising by 40%.
AFM Commander Clinton O’Neill said the surge in demand is what led to the major investment.
The new aircraft and helicopter will support a wide range of essential operations, including search and rescue missions, medical evacuations, border control, surveillance and intelligence gathering.
“Our direction is towards standardization,” O’Neill said. “We continue to build on what we have, and we also strengthen our product.”
With more pressure on Malta’s air capabilities, the upgrade is set to strengthen the country’s response both at sea and in the air.

Marco Micallef To Be Malta’s US Ambassador
Marco Micallef has been approved as Malta’s next Ambassador to the United States, following unanimous backing in Parliament’s Public Accounts Committee.
Micallef, a longtime aide to Foreign Affairs Minister Ian Borg, received cross-party support for the appointment and will now succeed Godfrey Xuereb, who has held the role since 2023.
Micallef currently serves as an advisor to Minister Borg and previously spent over four years as his Chief of Staff across multiple ministries.
He also played a coordinating role during Malta’s term on the United Nations Security Council and its 2024 chairmanship of the Organisation for Security and Cooperation in Europe.

Driver Arrested Outside St Julian’s Police Station After Suspected Drug Deal
A 22-year-old Serbian man has been arrested after allegedly attempting to carry out a drug handover just metres away from St Julian’s police station.
The motorist reportedly caught the attention of Drug Squad officers on patrol on Sunday due to suspicious behaviour, repeatedly picking up individuals, driving short distances and dropping them off shortly after.
Police continued monitoring the vehicle as it picked up another man in Triq San Ġorġ before stopping directly opposite the police station as reported by Times of Malta.
A search of the car revealed a drug sachet that was allegedly about to be passed to the passenger, leading to the driver’s immediate arrest.
The man was arraigned in court on Monday, where he pleaded not guilty to drug possession. No request for bail was made.
The prosecution was led by Police Inspector Francesco Mizzi along with lawyer Nadia Ciappara from the Attorney General’s office, while lawyers Shazoo Ghaznavi and Jessica Formosa appeared for the defence.

raw_statement:
Maltese Athletes Secure Athletics 5K National Crowns
Athletics Malta celebrated its athletes, Isaura Camilleri and Gabriel Farrugia, who have claimed the National 5K Road Running titles following strong performances at the latest Athletics Malta championship in Ta’ Qali.
The event also saw Farrugia set a new national 5K record while securing qualification for the CSSE Games. The Żurrieq Wolves athlete finished in 17:37, ahead of Pembroke’s Ritianne Zammit in 17:44, while Evolve’s Roberta Schembri placed third in 18:01.
In the men’s race, Gabriel Farrugia from Żurrieq Wolves achieved a double success, winning the 5K championship and securing the National Road Running League title for the first time in his career.
The 21-year-old recorded his fifth consecutive win this season, with a time of 14:26, which is now a senior and Under-23 national record for the distance. Simon Spiteri (St Patrick) finished in 14:33, taking one of the two CSSE Games qualifying spots alongside Farrugia, while Pembroke’s Luke Micallef came third in 14:43.
With the men’s title already decided in Farrugia’s favour, Aidan Tanti, Charlton Debono, Tommy Wallbank and Isaac Leo Attard will compete in the final race for second and third overall.
In contrast, the women’s league remains open, with Ritianne Zammit, Isaura Camilleri, Roberta Schembri and Rosalie Borg still in contention. The final 5K, organised by Mellieħa AC on 3rd May, will decide the women’s individual title.
In the club standings, Żurrieq Wolves have strengthened their lead, Mellieħa AC remain second, and Evolve Endurance Club are third. In the women’s team competition, Pembroke are set to retain their title, maintaining a strong lead over Evolve.
Elsewhere, Maltese athlete Mireya Bugeja won the women’s 1500- metre at the Wake Forest Invitational in North Carolina, US, clocking 4:23.22 and setting a new Under-23 record.
Congratulations to all!

New Homework Policy Unveiled To Focus On Balance And Meaningful Learning
A new national homework policy has been introduced in Malta with the aim of reshaping how children learn beyond the classroom.
The policy sets out structured guidelines on homework across different school levels, focusing on making assignments more meaningful, varied, and aligned with learning outcomes.
It emphasises that homework should support independent learning while encouraging students to develop responsibility and self-discipline.
It also stresses that homework should not create unnecessary stress or interfere with children’s wellbeing, co-curricular activities or overall balance in their daily lives.
The policy highlights the importance of homework quality over quantity, encouraging tasks that reinforce lessons, support diverse learning styles, and promote real-world application. It also calls for reduced reliance on adult help at home, aiming to strengthen independent study habits.
In addition, the framework aligns with national education principles and is designed to be reviewed periodically to ensure it remains relevant and effective over time.

Watch: Maltese Climber Reaches East Summit At Nepal’s Lobuche Mountain
Maltese mountaineer Nicky Ellul has successfully reached the East summit of Nepal’s Lobuche mountain (6,119 metres), marking a key stage in his acclimatisation programme ahead of his planned ascent of Mount Everest (8,849 metres).
Ellul began his summit push from Advanced Camp, situated at approximately 5,300 metres, at 2am on 19th April.
Climbing overnight in cold, high-altitude conditions, he reached the summit at 5.30am, completing a steady and controlled ascent before safely returning to Advanced Camp.
View this post on Instagram
After a short recovery period, he continued his descent to Lobuche (4,940 metres), demonstrating strong physical condition and effective acclimatisation. He is now at the Everest Base Camp (5,364 metres) to continue preparations for the next stage of his expedition.
The successful ascent of Lobuche East represents a significant milestone in Ellul’s journey, confirming his readiness to begin the rotation phase on Mount Everest, during which he will progressively climb to higher camps before attempting the final summit push.
Well done Nicky!
Credit: @nicky_ellul83 via Instagram

Tista' taqra bil- Malti.
A complaint is being prepared asking police to investigate an initiative by Dutch NGO Women on Waves, which has placed abortion pills in locked safes at multiple locations across Malta and Gozo.
The organisation says it has hidden 15 safes as part of a campaign to raise awareness about women’s rights. Each safe contains two types of pills intended to end a pregnancy, and the group says it will provide the location and code to pregnant women of under nine weeks who contact them.
Miriam Sciberras, CEO of the Life Network Foundation, confirmed to Newsbook Malta that her organisation is preparing a formal complaint requesting police investigation.
Sciberras described the pills as “lethal” and said the initiative posed risks to women’s health, adding that women in difficult circumstances needed help rather than “pills that eliminate”.
She also questioned why authorities had not acted independently, given that the safes are publicly accessible.
The importation of abortion pills into Malta is illegal. Concerns have also been raised about the safety of women who contact the organisation to obtain the pills.

Tista' taqra bil- Malti.
The first anniversary of Pope Francis’s death is being marked today with a rosary and mass at Rome’s Basilica of Santa Maria Maggiore, where the late pontiff was laid to rest.
Pope Francis died on 21 April 2025 – one day after Easter Sunday – at the age of 88, following a period of treatment at Rome’s Agostino Gemelli hospital for a respiratory infection. His final public appearance came on Easter Sunday, when he delivered the traditional Urbi et Orbi blessing and offered his last words to the world: “Happy Easter.”
The Cardinal Kevin Farrell announced his death at 7:35am the following day. An estimated 250,000 people paid their respects, and his funeral took place five days later.
According to Vatican News, the anniversary commemorations will begin at 5pm local time with the rosary in the Pauline Chapel – a place the late Pope visited frequently. A bronze commemorative plaque will then be unveiled, bearing a 160-character Latin inscription honouring Pope Francis’s special bond with the Marian icon Salus Populi Romani. The inscription notes that he prayed before the icon on 126 occasions.
Photos by Newsbook Malta’s photojournalist Miguela Xuereb
A mass will be celebrated at 6pm, during which a message from Pope Leo XIV – currently on an apostolic journey in Africa – will be read. The service will be shown on a large screen in Piazza Santa Maria Maggiore and broadcast on Vatican News.
A 26-minute documentary, featuring archive footage and symbolic sequences, will also be released, highlighting the pontificate of the “Pope of mercy” and the “Pope of the peripheries”.
Thirteen-year pontificate
Born Jorge Mario Bergoglio in Argentina, he was elected as the 266th Pope on 13 March 2013 following the resignation of Pope Benedict XVI.
During his 13-year pontificate, he presided over two Jubilees: the Jubilee of Mercy (2015-2016), emphasising forgiveness and compassion, and the Jubilee of Hope (2025), which was concluded by his successor. He also called for the Synod on Synodality in 2021, promoting greater dialogue and participation within the Church.
He authored four encyclicals, six apostolic exhortations, three apostolic constitutions and more than 50 motu proprios – over 100 official documents in total.

raw_statement:
Iran has told Pakistan, the principal mediator between Washington and Tehran, that it does not intend to participate in a new round of negotiations in Islamabad, dealing a potentially fatal blow to efforts to extend a ceasefire now less than 48 hours from expiry.
The announcement came after the US Navy guided missile destroyer USS Spruance intercepted the Iranian-flagged cargo vessel Touska in the Gulf of Oman in the early hours of Monday, after its crew refused to comply with orders to stop.
Donald Trump announced the seizure on Truth Social, writing that the Navy had stopped the ship “by blowing a hole in the engine room,” and that US Marines had taken control of the vessel. Iran described the seizure as “piracy.”
In reaction, Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei said Washington had “violated the ceasefire from the beginning of its implementation,” pointing to the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, imposed on 13 April, two days after the first round of Islamabad talks concluded without agreement, as well as the overnight ship capture.
He described the blockade as “unlawful and criminal,” and said it amounted to a “war crime and crime against humanity” under international law. Iran, he added, had formally notified Pakistan of these violations.
ran’s refusal is rooted in what it views as a consistent and predictable pattern: the United States and Israel attack while talking, violate ceasefires with impunity, and enter negotiations in bad faith.
Following the US-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon in November 2024, independent monitors recorded more than 4,500 Israeli violations within months, including strikes that killed civilians. In Gaza, Israeli forces continued military operations even after ceasefire announcements, killing more than 240 Palestinians in the month following one agreement. Israeli officials have publicly acknowledged that their military continues to operate for “defensive purposes” during so-called truces, a position Iran sees as confirmation that Israeli commitments are meaningless.
Prior to military actions against Iran in June 2025 and again on 28 February 2026, Washington publicly projected diplomatic optimism even as coordinated strikes were being prepared. From Tehran’s perspective, negotiations are not a path to resolution but a tactic to buy time, reposition forces, and maintain the element of surprise. Critics describe the approach as “talks, then bombs.”
Iranian scepticism extends to the composition of the US negotiating team. Both Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were described by a British diplomat with knowledge of the Geneva talks as “Israeli assets that dragged a president into a war he wants to get out of,” a characterisation echoed by a Gulf diplomat. For Iran, this confirms that Washington cannot be an honest broker: its negotiators prioritise Israeli strategic objectives over American interests, and diplomacy reliably precedes rather than prevents military action.
Tehran said the US had arrived at the first round of Islamabad talks with four maximalist demands presented as non-negotiable, including a permanent zero-enrichment policy and the dismantling of Iran’s regional alliances, while offering only limited financial relief and refusing to lift core sanctions. Iranian officials described the session not as a genuine negotiation but as an attempt to impose surrender terms.
Despite Tehran’s position, Trump announced that vice president JD Vance, accompanied by Witkoff and Kushner – the same team that led the first round – would fly to Islamabad, with Vance confirmed by the White House to land on Tuesday. US government aircraft carrying communications equipment and motorcade support had already landed at Pakistan’s Nur Khan air base by Sunday.
But Trump’s accompanying statements did little to ease tensions. He warned that if no deal was reached, the US would “knock out every single power plant and every single bridge in Iran,” while simultaneously telling Fox News that an agreement would be signed “today,” a claim Iranian state media dismissed as “a media game.”
Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said a “deep historical mistrust” pervades Tehran’s view of Washington. “They seek Iran’s surrender,” he wrote. “The people of Iran will not bow to coercion.”
Pakistan said it remained cautiously hopeful of bringing both parties back to the table. Pakistani prime minister Shehbaz Sharif held a 45-minute call with Pezeshkian on Monday, briefing him on recent visits to Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Türkiye.
Russia urged both sides to maintain the ceasefire. Israeli strikes, meanwhile, continued to hit towns in southern Lebanon despite the truce nominally remaining in force, with Lebanese civil defence recovering two bodies from the Qasmiyah Bridge on Monday.

Former opposition leader Adrian Delia has told the Speaker of the House he is prepared to hand over his mobile phone and all correspondence following a complaint that he received threatening messages from the partner of a candidate for an ambassadorial post.
The Nationalist Party MP raised a breach of privilege complaint on Monday after receiving what he described as threatening messages from the partner of Roseanne Camilleri, who appeared before the Public Appointments Committee last Wednesday as a nominee for the role of non-resident ambassador to Liechtenstein.
According to Delia, one of the messages read: “Even the devil can quote scripture.”
The messages followed a committee hearing in which Delia questioned Camilleri extensively over reports of cost overruns relating to the refurbishment of health centres in Birkirkara and Qormi, which occurred during her tenure as CEO of Primary Health Care.
Camilleri told the committee she had reported the matter to the Permanent Secretary within the Health Ministry.
Speaker Anġlu Farrugia asked Delia to pass over all correspondence and messages he received. The MP has agreed to hand over everything, including his phone, to allow the Speaker to verify the veracity of the complaint.
The committee also heard Marco Micallef, nominated for the role of resident ambassador to the United States.

raw_statement:
A masked hunter who was jailed after attempting to escape arrest by throwing away his shotgun and pulling a police sergeant downhill, causing minor injuries, during a confrontation with environmental officers in Xgħajra, was captured on camera by BirdLife Malta.
The incident occurred on Saturday when a BirdLife Malta Spring Watch team filmed an individual actively pursuing turtle-doves under Fort St Leonard. Officers from the Environmental Protection Unit (EPU) arrived on site and caught the hunter in the act.
The footage published by BirdLife shows the individual attempting to flee and resist arrest, discarding his shotgun and pulling a police sergeant downhill, according to the conservation group. The officer sustained minor injuries.
The hunter was subsequently jailed due to previous offences, including other cases of hunting without a licence previously reported by BirdLife Malta.
Widespread illegal targeting
The arrest came during the first week of Malta’s spring hunting season, which officially permits only the hunting of common quail. However, BirdLife Malta estimates that 72% of hunters observed over six mornings between Monday 13 April and Saturday 18 April were instead primarily targeting turtle-doves.
The estimate is based on systematic observations from strategic watchpoints across Malta and Gozo, where trained staff distinguished between quail hunters actively walking through open vegetation – often with dogs – and those positioned in hides, towers or stationary setups typically associated with turtle-dove hunting.
In parallel, BirdLife says it documented multiple incidents of illegal activity including the use of live or artificial decoys, electronic callers to attract migrating birds, and coordinated shooting following radio communication between hunters regarding incoming birds.
A shot turtle-dove was also recovered from Ġebel Ciantar on Friday afternoon and admitted for rehabilitation. A veterinary examination confirmed the presence of lead pellets and a dislocated shoulder.
In several other cases, hunters fled the scene upon police arrival. Video evidence of multiple incidents has been submitted to the EPU for further investigation.
‘Systemic failure’
While the Xgħajra case involved an unlicensed hunter, BirdLife Malta notes that most illegal turtle-dove hunting is carried out by licensed individuals.
“It is evident year after year that there is no realistic way to ensure hunters restrict themselves to just quail once the season opens,” said Nicholas Barbara, head of conservation at BirdLife Malta.
“What we keep witnessing is a systemic failure of regulation and enforcement. There is no effective self-regulation within much of the hunting community when it comes to turtle-doves, and species-specific enforcement is practically impossible.”
With just under 8,000 licences issued this season, BirdLife questions whether adequate enforcement is feasible given the limited resources afforded to the EPU and its absence from Gozo, where police presence is typically triggered only by NGO reports.
Red-listed species
The European turtle-dove is classified as vulnerable and red-listed, with populations in steep decline across the continent. Under the EU’s Birds Directive, spring hunting is generally prohibited to protect birds returning to breed, but Malta repeatedly invokes derogations to bypass these rules.
“Claims that the 1,500 turtle-dove quota is controlled are simply false,” Barbara added. Fewer than 10% of hunters use the existing reporting mechanism.
BirdLife Malta is calling on the European Commission to closely scrutinise the derogation reports submitted by Malta’s Wild Birds Regulation Unit and has invited Commission representatives to visit Malta during the spring hunting period to join its monitoring operations.

raw_statement:
A batch of luxury smoked salmon has been urgently recalled over fears it may be contaminated with Listeria monocytogenes, the Food Safety and Security Authority has announced.
The affected product is “Salmone Affumicato Norvegese con pistacchio”, sold under the Deluxe brand in 100g packs. It bears lot number LC22606501 and has a “use by” date of 21 April 2026.
Consumers are warned not to eat the product, and are advised to dispose of it or return it to the store.
Listeria infection can cause mild flu-like or gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea, vomiting and diarrhoea. However, in vulnerable groups, including pregnant women, babies, the elderly and those with weakened immune systems, it can lead to serious complications. The incubation period can stretch from three to 70 days, meaning symptoms may not appear for weeks after consumption.

raw_statement:
The Nationalist Party has called for urgent action at Malta International Airport, where the rollout of the European Union’s new digital border system is causing queues of up to 40 minutes and prompting Ryanair to warn it may redirect seat capacity away from the island ahead of the summer season.
Shadow ministers for tourism and home affairs, Mario de Marco and Darren Carabott, accused the Labour administration of a failure of planning that now threatens to damage one of Malta’s most economically critical sectors.
They urged immediate action before peak summer travel begins, including hiring additional border control officers, expanding processing kiosks, investing in staff training, and conducting a rapid review of airport procedures.
“Not for the first time, the government has been found lacking in planning,” the PN statement read. “Failure to act will lead to chaos at the airport.”
The disruption stems from the phased introduction across Schengen member states of the EU’s Entry/Exit System, a digital border management scheme requiring biometric data collection and advanced passport scanning for all non-EU nationals.
While designed to strengthen external border security, the additional processing time per passenger has created significant bottlenecks at Malta International Airport, where queues have reached 40 minutes during busy periods.
One passenger last week reported waiting 20 minutes aboard a tarmac bus due to congestion at passport control before even reaching the terminal.
In June 2024, Newsbook Malta had revealed the home affairs ministry admitted to a “race against time” to meet the October deadline, risking potential Schengen suspension due to system incompatibility. Subsequently Newsbook Malta reported MEP Peter Agius’s warnings of a “real risk” of suspension as the government prepared a temporary manual system.
The consequences have drawn a direct warning from Ryanair. Malta Air chief executive David O’Brien wrote to home affairs Minister Byron Camilleri seeking assurances that border controls would be fully staffed for summer.
“If we find ourselves with significant congestion and delay, we would have to redirect capacity away from Malta to other destinations,” O’Brien said, adding that Europe was “utterly unprepared in a general sense” for the system’s demands. The UK, Malta’s single largest tourism market, is among the non-EU nationalities most affected, as British travellers now fall within the EES remit following Brexit.
KM Malta executive chairman David Curmi has separately warned that delays could trigger costly passenger compensation claims payable by airlines even when hold-ups originate with border control systems. Both carriers have backed industry calls for a temporary EES suspension during peak summer months.
Malta International Airport chief executive Alan Borg said the airport was working with police to improve processing times and adding immigration desks.
Tourism minister Ian Borg confirmed the government was in talks with the European Commission and the United Kingdom but said Malta was not considering suspending implementation.
Meanwhile, the home affairs ministry said existing resources were already operating at full capacity, a response the Opposition described as wholly inadequate given the scale of the threat to the tourism season.
speaker: unknown

raw_statement:
The Archdiocese of Malta has denied allegations published by The Shift News concerning the conversion of an ODZ farmhouse for Archbishop Charles Scicluna.
In a statement, the Archdiocese categorically rejected what it called “false, defamatory and misleading claims” in an article titled ‘Anger within Curia over costly conversion of ODZ farmhouse for Archbishop Scicluna’.
The article claimed the property was being used by the Archbishop for “private gatherings with friends” at weekends. The Archdiocese said this was “entirely untrue”, adding that neither the Archbishop nor anyone else had organised or held gatherings there.
It said the property, located on the outskirts of Rabat, is currently not habitable. The decision to renovate it was taken to prevent further disrepair and potential misuse, including the risk of abandonment or illegal occupation.
The Archdiocese dismissed suggestions that expenditure on the works was “excessive”, describing such claims as “unfounded and irresponsible”. It said the interventions were necessary on a property in poor condition, involving no luxury finishes or interior designers.
It accused The Shift News of selectively interpreting information provided by the Curia to fit “a preconceived narrative”, adding that the approach was consistent with previous reporting on Church-related issues.
“Any suggestion that the Archdiocese is indulging in lavish or unnecessary spending is entirely misleading. On the contrary, every effort is being made to contain costs and ensure that only essential works are undertaken.”
While noting that several other claims made in the article are likewise false or misleading, the Archdiocese stressed that “reporting must be grounded in factual accuracy and responsible journalism, and not based on unsubstantiated or misleading allegations aimed at causing reputational harm to the Curia and its leadership.”
The Archdiocese said it reserved the right to take further steps to safeguard its reputation.
speaker: unknown

The 40-year-old man who tragically lost his life in a refuse truck accident in Qormi early this morning has been identified as Jurgen Cassar.

A magisterial inquiry led by Magistrate Monica Borg Galea has been launched, while the Occupational Health and Safety Authority is also conducting a separate investigation.

First Ever Airport Hotel To Open Next Year In Malta
SkyParks General Manager explains that they’re expecting to build Malta’s first airport hotel next year, named ‘SkyParks 2’, expecting it to be a success with business travellers.
With 98 rooms, swimming pools, spa facilities, meeting rooms and a restaurant spread across nine floors, prices will start from €110 and go up to €200 per night for a room, describing it as an affordable designer finish, Times of Malta reported.
He explained how SkyParks is going full on the hotel development before shifting operations onto constructing two office blocks. The new office blocks will provide 20,000 square metres of office space, which will cater for 2,000 employees.
The buildings will also feature commercial outlets similar to those at the already existing office block and new ones, including a hairdresser, beautician, a grab-and-go market.
The general manager said that this will solve a problem of many travellers who would oftentimes sleep at the airport. The hotel will also feature 500 underground parking spaces, whilst catering for cab services as most business travellers are more likely to use cab services than drive their own car.
The hotel will have an unobstructed panoramic view of the airport as well as the surrounding localities, offering guests a fantastic experience with its rooftop and indoor pool, as well as a cocktail bar.

Spin, Sweat, Rave: Malta Just Hosted Its First Cycle & Rave
Malta just got a brand new kind of Sunday plan, and it might honestly be the future of partying.
The island’s first-ever Cycle & Rave brought together high-energy spin sessions and a full-on morning rave at Dr. Juice’s Quad Central location, kicking off at 8am on Sunday. The event started with three 20-minute waves of Drive Fitness Studio’s signature cycling classes, all set to pumping dance music mixed live by Dr. Juice founder John Winfield.
But the vibe didn’t stop when the bikes did. As soon as participants wrapped up their sessions, they spilled out into the rest of the venue, which quickly transformed into a laid-back but buzzing brunch rave. Think protein shakes, health shots and fresh bites, paired with a three-hour DJ set and a crowd that clearly wasn’t ready to go home.
View this post on Instagram
For Drive, the event was all about taking their community beyond the studio, blending fitness with music and connection in a way that felt both energising and social. And judging by the turnout, it worked, plans for a second edition are already in the works, potentially in a new location.
At the same time, the event marked the launch of a new concept by Dr. Juice called DJ Club, focused on bringing people together through movement, music and food. As Winfield put it, it’s about creating a space for “fierce, fun, connective energy” that can take different forms and pop up anywhere.
From early-morning spin to full-on dancefloor energy, Cycle & Rave proved that wellness and nightlife don’t have to be separate worlds, and Malta might just be onto something big.

raw_statement:
BirdLife Malta Reports Multiple Turtle Doves Shot Illegally In First Week Of Spring
Data collected by BirdLife Malta during the first week of this year’s spring hunting season indicates that an estimated 72% of observed hunters were not targeting Common Quail but were instead primarily aiming at Turtle-dove.
The estimate is based on systematic observations carried out by Spring Watch teams across Malta and Gozo.
Monitoring took place from strategic watchpoints, where trained staff and volunteers distinguished between quail hunters—typically moving through open vegetation, often with dogs—and individuals positioned in hides, towers, or other stationary setups commonly associated with Turtle-dove hunting.
Observations were conducted over six mornings between 13th April and 18th April. The figures are described as conservative, particularly in areas such as Miżieb and Aħrax, where dense woodland limits visibility.
In parallel, BirdLife Malta reported multiple incidents to the police which, it says, point to widespread illegal targeting of Turtle-doves.
These include the use of live or artificial decoys, electronic callers to attract migrating birds, and coordinated shooting following radio communication between hunters regarding incoming birds.
A shot Turtle-dove was recovered from Ġebel Ciantar on Friday afternoon and admitted for rehabilitation. A veterinary examination confirmed the presence of lead pellets and a dislocated shoulder.
According to BirdLife Malta, in several cases hunters fled upon police arrival. Video evidence of multiple incidents has been submitted to the Environmental Protection Unit (EPU) for further investigation.
On Saturday, a BirdLife Malta team in Xgħajra filmed a masked individual allegedly pursuing Turtle-doves under Fort St Leonard. EPU officers arrived on site and apprehended the individual.
The person reportedly attempted to escape and resist arrest, discarding a shotgun and pulling a police sergeant downhill, resulting in minor injuries.
The individual has since been jailed due to previous offences, including cases of hunting without a licence also reported by BirdLife Malta.
While this case involved an unlicensed hunter, BirdLife Malta stated that most illegal Turtle-dove hunting is carried out by licensed individuals, despite the first week being officially restricted to quail hunting.
“It is evident year after year, that there is no realistic way to ensure hunters restrict themselves to just quail once the season opens,” said Nicholas Barbara, Head of Conservation at BirdLife Malta. “What we keep witnessing is a systemic failure of regulation and enforcement. There is no effective self-regulation within much of the hunting community when it comes to Turtle-doves, and species-specific enforcement is practically impossible.”
With just under 8,000 licences issued this season, BirdLife Malta noted that a ratio of seven police officers per 1,000 licences would be required in practice.
The organisation questioned whether such deployment is feasible, citing limited resources within the EPU of the Malta Police Force and its absence from Gozo, where police presence is typically triggered by NGO reports. According to BirdLife Malta, this leaves large areas unmonitored, allowing illegal activity to persist.
Barbara also stated that claims regarding control of the 1,500 Turtle-dove quota are inaccurate. Although a reporting mechanism exists, fewer than 10% of hunters are said to use it.
BirdLife Malta further noted that spring hunting involves thousands of hunters in dense concentrations, with high volumes of shooting reported even from single locations as Turtle-doves pass through lines of occupied hides and towers across both islands.
Footage of such activity during the first week has been published in a video on YouTube.
Barbara added that, given the scale and density of hunting activity, Turtle-doves are allegedly killed illegally during the first week, followed by largely unreported and uncontrolled killing during the remaining two weeks of the spring season.
BirdLife Malta has called on the European Commission to scrutinise derogation reports submitted by Malta’s Wild Birds Regulation Unit (WBRU), and has invited representatives to visit Malta during the spring hunting period and observe its monitoring operations.
The organisation stated that Turtle-dove hunting should have an expiry, noting that the species is red-listed and nearing extinction, and that hunting during pre-nuptial migration is prohibited under the Birds Directive.

Former Labour Party (PL) Secretary General Jimmy Magro has had a prison sentence of four years and eight months confirmed by the Court of Appeal in a high-profile corruption case.
This means that Magro will now serve a prison term, making him one of the most senior political party officials in recent years to be sentenced to imprisonment.
Magro was found guilty of corruption-related charges, including using his influence to commit a criminal offence.
The case dates back to 2014, when Magro was serving as a special consultant within the Local Councils Association. He was accused of requesting a €25,000 payment from a private company in exchange for assistance in securing a government contract worth €250,000 for waste-collection machinery.
The Court heard evidence of emails sent by Magro from his personal Gmail account outside office hours, in which he contacted prospective bidder Victor Bonello to discuss what he described as the project’s “commercial take”.
The Court dismissed Magro’s explanations, stating that the phrase referred to potential gains for both the bidder and the association, and added that his explanations “make no sense and leave no doubt as to how untruthful they are”.
The judgment, delivered by Madam Justice Consuelo Scerri Herrera, rejected Magro’s appeal and reaffirmed the seriousness of a public official abusing his position for personal gain.
Magro, who served as Labour Party Secretary General for 12 years until 2003, consistently denied the allegations. After stepping down from the role, he was appointed to various government positions when the party was elected to government in 2013.
Despite the corruption allegations, Magro remained employed by the government entity Malta Enterprise until reaching the age of 62, and was even allowed to receive €9,000 in back payments that had been withheld due to his suspension.
The prosecution in this case was led by Police Inspector Wayne Rodney Borg.

A 42-year-old man was jailed for three and a half years after he admitted to a violent robbery at a Paceville bar in 2024 in a plea bargaining agreement.
The robbery took place early in the morning on 6 October 2024 – a Sunday – after the bar had closed for the night.
Viktor Marković, a Serbian national, entered the bar through the service door, wearing a mask to disguise his identity.
He came across a cleaner who was alone and demanded cash. The cleaner could not cooperate, having no idea where the money was being kept, and suffered slight injuries at the hands of Marković eventually found nearly €6,000 in cash at an office and fled the scene.
Police however solve the case after confirming the suspect’s use of a car, and Marković was arrested in Gozo two days later.
Marković faced no less than 14 charges, including theft aggravated by violence, means, time and value, slight bodily harm, holding the cleaner against his will, joyriding and no less than four separate charges related to the breaching of bail conditions.
While he initially pleaded not guilty, a plea bargaining agreement was reached on 14 April, which saw Marković plead guilty to all charges while the prosecution and defence agreed to recommend three and a half years’ imprisonment. The agreement also saw the two parties agree that Marković should not forfeit the entirety of the deposits and personal guarantees he had made to secure bail.
Such agreements are not binding on the court, but magistrate Ann Marie Thake saw no reason not to follow the recommendation as she handed down her sentence on Tuesday.
Marković was thus jailed, and ordered to forfeit €1,900 in bail deposits he had made.
He was also ordered to pay the bar operators €5,911.10, representing the amount stolen, and an additional €10,207 in court expenses.
A three-year protection order was also issued in favour of the cleaner and the bar owner.

The fuel crisis created by the war launched by US and Israel against Iran is leading the Association of Tractor and Trailer Operators (ATTO) to express serious concerns about Malta’s logistics chain, not least in neighbouring Italy.
ATTO said on Tuesday that it was observing “early but intensifying signs of strain across selected European fuel corridors, with particular pressure emerging in Italy’s road haulage sector.”
The operators it represents reported sustained diesel price inflation, with Italian retail prices exceeding €2 per litre, leading to a significant escalation of costs across the trailer and freight industry. Diesel, it pointed out, typically accounted for 30-40% of operating costs, and accordingly, sharp price rises were placing extreme pressure on the industry.
Another concern is the prospect of fuel shortages further down the line, with ATTO chair Joseph Bugeja stating that the early signs of disruption were becoming more evident.
“Alongside sustained cost pressures, we are seeing isolated instances of fuel supply constraints in parts of Italy. While not yet systemic, these warrant close monitoring,” he said.
Various operators affiliated with ATTO confirmed that they were facing increasing operational strain, including steep fuel price rises and the occasional shortages.
But while a link between rising prices and the impact of the Iran war – not least the closure of the Strait of Hormuz through which a significant share of the world’s oil and gas ordinarily transits – associations are raising another concern: that the diesel price escalation may also be driven by speculative pricing dynamics, “amplifying volatility beyond underlying market fundamentals and intensifying financial strain across operators.”
Another key issue is the Italian haulage sector – a crucial part of Malta’s logistics chain as the key link to Europe – which is facing liquidity pressure due to rising costs and 60-90 payment cycles, which raise the risk of rate renegotiations, service reductions, and potential operational disruption.
ATTO also noted that pricing transmission across Europe remains uneven due to differing taxation systems and policy interventions, contributing to corridor volatility.
In this context, Bugeja emphasised Malta’s structural exposure to these risks as an import-dependent island economy.
“With no overland alternatives, Malta is entirely dependent on the reliability of European transport corridors. Around 55,000 trailer movements annually underpin national trade flows, and any sustained disruption, whether it’s cost-driven or physical, can rapidly impact economic stability,” he said.
ATTO said that it would continue to monitor developments closely and engage stakeholders where necessary to safeguard supply chain continuity.

raw_statement:
Momentum confirmed its opposition to what it described as a “monstrous” overpass planned to connect Swieqi and Pembroke, decrying the plans as an example of government incompetence which failed to address residents’ needs.
Through planning application PA/1595/26, Infrastructure Malta is proposing a pedestrian bridge over Triq Sant’Andrija right next to two Pembroke schools: the National Sports School and the St Catherine’s High School.
The agency consulted with the Pembroke local council, whose mayor Kaylon Zammit backs the project, but left the Swieqi local council and its residents in the dark.
The application has since attracted some 300 objections, including from the Swieqi local council and various residents of the locality. The council is holding a protest on Saturday, 25 April: a protect which Momentum is encouraging people to join.
‘Another example of government incompetence’
The party and its leader Arnold Cassola – a Swieqi resident – weighed in on the proposal on Tuesday, questioning the planned imposition of what is effectively “a four-storey concrete structure” without any proper planning or consultation.
It highlighted similar concerns about the planned pedestrian crossing in Msida, with Cassola decrying the prioritisation of cars over everything else.
“This project is another example of government incompetence. A major road was recently built, prioritising cars, yet no serious thought was given to pedestrian safety. Now, instead of fixing this failure properly, the only solution being presented is a massive overpass that will permanently scar the area.”
The party said that it was concerning that other alternatives – not least a pedestrian underpass – were not properly considered. It observed that underpasses were a proven solution, with successful examples including various pedestrian crossings in Floriana and at the Birkirkara bypass.
“These were built decades ago and are still valid, yet today’s government prefers oversized, intrusive structures,” Momentum observed.
In this context, the party is proposing an underpass instead, as a “safer, more practical and less visually intrusive solution.”
“Following the Msida pedestrian crossing saga, unfortunately, this is yet another case of ignoring expert advice and highlights a serious failure in governance,” Cassola said. “Residents were not properly consulted, transparency is lacking, and key assessments, including visual impact and pedestrian usability, have not been presented.”
The choice of location has been a key point of criticism of the proposed overpass, given its location at a distance from key residential and commercial areas of both communities.
The existing pedestrian crossing some 400m away, in contrast, is right next to the Luxol Ground on the Pembroke side and the de facto commercial centre of Swieqi around the lower reaches of Triq tal-Ibraġ. The planning application gives no indication that this well-used crossing will in any way be impacted by the planned overpass.

Tista' taqra bil- Malti.
Pope Leo XIV headed to Equatorial Guinea on Tuesday morning for the fourth and final leg of his apostolic journey in Africa, which has also seen him visit Algeria, Cameroon and Angola.
Equatorial Guinea is one of Africa’s smallest countries, with a population of around 1.8 million people.
It includes a mainland region sandwiched between Cameroon and Gabon and various islands, most notably Bioko, the site of its former capital Malabo: the newly-built Ciudad de la Paz was made the capital just this year. It also includes the small island of Annobón, over 500km away from the rest of the country, to the south of the island nation of São Tomé and Principe.
The country’s territory is a legacy of its past as a Spanish colony: it was known as Spanish Guinea before gaining independence in 1968 and Spanish remains the main official language. As a result of its colonial history, the country’s population is overwhelmingly Catholic.
Leo XIV is the second pope to visit the country – Pope John Paul II had visited in 1982 – and the country has changed significantly since then, becoming one of the largest oil producers in sub-Saharan Africa and one of the continent’s wealthiest.
But the poverty witnessed by the late pope over 4 decades ago is still very much a reality, the result of extreme wealth inequality, and the country remains firmly in the grip of the same dictator who led the country back then: the 83-year-old Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo.
The pope landed in Malabo, where he will spend most of his time in the country. He will travel to the mainland on Wednesday but will return back to the city and celebrate a mass at the Malabo Stadium before leaving for Rome on Thursday.

European Commissioner for Environment, Water Resilience and a Competitive Circular Economy Jessika Roswall visited WasteServ, where she was shown the significant progress being made in Malta’s waste management sector and the ongoing efforts to strengthen the country’s transition towards a circular economy.
The visit was held together with Minister for the Environment, Energy and Public Cleanliness Miriam Dalli and WasteServ CEO Richard Bilocca. Together, they toured key WasteServ facilities, including the ECOHIVE Complex in Naxxar.
Commissioner Roswall began her visit at the ECOHIVE Academy in Marsaskala, where she was introduced to WasteServ’s educational programmes. Through interactive and engaging learning experiences, the Academy promotes waste management and circular economy principles in a fun and accessible way, particularly for children and young people. The Commissioner noted the importance of education, especially at a young age, in demonstrating how waste can be transformed into a valuable resource.
The Commissioner was then given an overview of the waste management infrastructure projects that have already been implemented, as well as those currently in the pipeline across WasteServ’s operations, with particular focus on the ECOHIVE Project.
Once fully implemented, the ECOHIVE Project will transform the way Malta manages its waste, significantly reducing landfilling while creating valuable resources. The visit also served as an opportunity to highlight how tangible operational improvements, combined with long-term strategic planning, are reshaping waste management practices across the country.
“It was inspiring to visit on-site projects in Malta that promote the transition to circularity and I have seen on the ground how waste can be turned into a resource. This is not only important for Malta but also an opportunity that Europe has to seize. The circular economy is our path to build resilience, especially given the scale of conflicts around the world to access raw materials and the very big geopolitical shift taking place around us. Circularity is the only way to future-proof our economy for the challenges ahead. We depend on other countries for more than 90% of critical raw materials, but only recycle less than 1%. That is why we will present the Circular Economy Act later this year, this will be the European framework that will allow us to build a genuine single market for circular materials,” said Commissioner Roswall.
Minister Miriam Dalli added: “The investments introduced by this Government and the policies implemented over the past years are proving to be a real game changer. We are not only managing waste more efficiently but also transforming it into valuable resources. Today’s visit underlined WasteServ’s commitment to continuous improvement, innovation and education as key drivers in achieving sustainable waste management and supporting Malta’s wider environmental and climate objectives.”
Commenting on the visit, WasteServ CEO Richard Bilocca said: “WasteServ is fully committed to delivering a better environment for future generations. Education, particularly among children, remains central to our efforts. Our children are the motivation behind the daily work carried out at our facilities, and we are determined to leave them a better environment than the one we inherited. Through continued investment and the dedication of our teams, we remain fully focused on delivering Malta’s ambitious waste management strategy.”
During her visit, Commissioner Roswall was also shown the progress being made on the project to transform the former Sant’Antnin waste management facility into 24,000 square metres of green public open space, representing a total investment of €37 million.

Tista' taqra bil- Malti.
Controversial contractor Mark Gaffarena received a suspended sentence and a €32,000 fine on Tuesday after he was found guilty of illegally keeping a number of dangerous animals at two separate sites.
Gaffarena earned notoriety for a scandal which had forced the resignation of then-parliamentary secretary Michael Falzon – now minister – in 2016: a controversial Valletta expropriation deal which appeared tailor-made to benefit him directly. The deal has been revoked by the courts, and Gaffarena is one of various individuals undergoing criminal proceedings over the matter.
The case which led to his conviction on Tuesday dates back to 2023 and concerns the illegal keeping of various animals in Qormi and Żebbuġ.
Gaffarena had built an illegal villa in an ODZ site in Żebbuġ years ago – which remains standing – and in 2018 he had actually applied to sanction the construction of “a residence and zoo.” That effort failed, and earlier this year, the PA was also unimpressed by his attempts to have the villa approved as a “sheep farm.”
In any case, Gaffarena faced a total of nine charges over the improper keeping of exotic and dangerous animals at both sites, in proceedings that followed a failed attempt to regularise his situation.
Gaffarena had sought to make use of a 2016 government amnesty, applying to register a puma, a lion, four monkeys and a lemur.
But his application was turned down after inspections which revealed that Gaffarena had various other unregistered animals, kept in inadequate conditions.
A 2019 inspection in Gaffarena’s Żebbuġ property found a puma, seven monkeys, a lemur and three tigers.
Another inspection was carried out in 2023, and this time round, inspectors found only the tigers and puma, in an inadequate enclosure in poor state of repair in Żebbuġ. At the site in Qormi, meanwhile, they found two lions, three leopards, tigers brought over from Żebbuġ and a number of monkeys.
Criminal proceedings followed, and Gaffarena was acquitted of a single charge related to the keeping of green monkeys because the evidence supplied lacked detail and was deemed inadequate.
For all other animals, however – 13 in total – the evidence proved overwhelming, and Gaffarena was found guilty of all other 8 charges.
Magistrate condemns mistreatment of animals acquired for personal enjoyment
Magistrate Donatella Frendo Dimech took aim at Gaffarena’s mistreatment of the exotic animals he brought before delivering her sentence, noting how the contractor failed to address the situation despite the many years in which he was made aware of the shortcomings.
She criticised him for “acquiring the animals for his personal enjoyment, giving no thought about their wellbeing,” noting how the animals were left in small places “fighting for food and living in neglect because of filth and the ingress of water.”
The magistrate also took into account Gaffarena’s criminal record as she decided on a suspended sentence – a 17-month jail term suspended for 30 months – and a €32,000 fine.
The animals are being confiscated at Gaffarena’s expense, while Gaffarena is being banned from applying for a permit to keep animals for 15 years.
Inspector Elliot Magro prosecuted.

raw_statement:
Malta’s entrepreneurs and small businesses can soon discover how European Union regulation shapes the business environment – and how they can use that knowledge to strengthen and future-proof their enterprises.
EU Unlocked – Law, Tech and Growth for Entrepreneurs is tailored to the realities faced by business owners operating within a small EU Member State. Jointly launched by the European Parliament Liaison Office in Valletta and the Foundation for the Promotion of Entrepreneurial Initiatives (FPEI), the course will take place across three Friday morning sessions in May.
Each practical, policy-focused module provides participants with the tools to understand regulation, identify opportunities and respond strategically to change at a European level.
The programme’s opening session covers how EU laws are made, guiding participants through legislative procedures, negotiation dynamics and the pathways through which businesses and SMEs can influence European policy. Subsequent sessions explore the funding instruments, training programmes and cross-border initiatives the EU offers to support business growth and innovation.
Participants will also be introduced to the proposed 28th regime – a new EU-wide legal framework aimed at simplifying operations for fast-growing companies – alongside practical guidance on taxation and licensing within EU and national frameworks.
The course examines how digitalisation and AI are reshaping SMEs, including the opportunities they present for innovation and productivity, as well as the responsibilities businesses have in protecting workers’ rights under the Just Transition Directive. A final module looks at how geopolitical tensions, global uncertainty, supply chain disruptions and international relations affect markets and entrepreneurial decision-making.
“Entrepreneurs want insights they can apply immediately,” says Giselle Borg Olivier, Foundation Manager at FPEI. “This course is about giving business owners the clarity and confidence to engage with EU policy, spot opportunities others might miss and make informed decisions that set their businesses up for sustainable, long-term growth in an ever-changing European and global environment.”
Dr Mario Sammut, Head of the European Parliament Liaison Office, adds that “EU Unlocked is also about bringing Europe closer to small businesses and to the individuals heading those small businesses. The course will provide a space to candidly discuss
European issues affecting them while giving them tools on how to engage and shape their future in the EU.”
EU Unlocked – Law, Tech and Growth for Entrepreneurs takes place on 15, 22 and 29 May 2026, from 9.30am to 12.30pm, at the European Parliament Liaison Office in Valletta. Registration for each session is available at fpei.mt/events-fpei/.

raw_statement:
Watch: Two Goodbyes In Seven Weeks- A Family’s Story With Hospice Malta
Caroline Saliba shared how Hospice Malta helped her after losing both her parents within seven weeks.
“A year ago we lost our mother. Then, seven and a half weeks later, we lost our father,” she said.
Her mother, Eucharista Saliba Sultana, passed away on 8th March 2025, followed by her father, Giovanni Saliba, on 1st May 2025. In between, the family faced the daily realities familiar to many others: appointments, exhaustion, medication schedules, sleepless nights and the fear of not doing enough. It was during this period that Hospice Malta became part of their everyday life.
Caroline recalls the small but meaningful moments: a nurse or doctor taking time to sit down, a kind word at the right moment, and the reassurance that they were no longer facing everything alone.
“I feel that if it wasn’t for Hospice that gave us support and energy to continue looking after our mother, when I say Hospice, I mean all the therapists, doctors who helped my mother and father. We certainly would not have been able to give our parents the level of care that we did… For sure,” she said.
She added that the support made comfort possible. “There’s nothing on my conscience… we were able to do certain things because Hospice was there to help us- otherwise we certainly wouldn’t have been able to make them comfortable… to that level.”
Hospice Malta provides free, specialised palliative care focused on comfort, dignity, and quality of life, supporting both patients and their families with medical, therapeutic, and psychosocial services.
For Caroline, this also meant being able to honour her father’s wish to remain at home. He died peacefully there, something she says would not have been possible without Hospice support. “With their help, we could make it happen.”
Her experience is deeply personal, but not uncommon.
More than 1,500 families are supported by Hospice Malta every year, with demand continuing to rise. Almost 1,000 new end-of-life patients sought help last year, while in 2025 an average of three new families a day contacted the NGO.
View this post on Instagram
In the same period, Hospice delivered 33,000 home professional sessions and over 4,000 social and psychosocial sessions, while more than 400 patients attended its Day Therapy Unit and clinics. Chairperson Bernadette Bonnici Kind said: “More families are reaching out to us every year. The telethon helps ensure we can continue to be there for everyone who needs us, free of charge.”
Hospice Malta will host its annual 12-hour live fundraising telethon this coming 25th April, from 11am to 11pm. The broadcast will bring together patients, families, healthcare professionals, artists, and the wider community in support of Hospice Malta’s mission to provide free palliative care across Malta and Gozo.
Funds raised during the telethon will support Hospice Malta’s expanding services, including community care and day therapy programmes, as well as the newly inaugurated St Michael Hospice headquarters, which includes a state-of-the-art 16-room In-Patient Unit designed to offer round-the-clock medical care, comfort, and dignity when it is needed most.

Teen Denied Bail Over Golden Bay Brawl That Left 21-Year-Old Injured
An 18-year-old man has been denied bail after being charged in connection with a fight at Golden Bay that left a 21-year-old man from Paola with grievous injuries.
Mohammed Haj, a Syrian national living in Marsa, was arrested on Sunday at around 6pm following a reported brawl involving around 10 people at the popular Mellieħa beach as reported by Times of Malta.
Police and Rapid Intervention Unit officers were called to the scene by the Malta Ranger Unit, where they found a group attempting to flee by climbing the rocky side of the bay.
Haj was among those trying to escape but was the only individual who stopped when ordered by police, leading to his arrest.
He has been charged with causing grievous bodily harm, threatening violence, assault and breaching the public peace. Haj pleaded not guilty.
In court, his lawyer stated that the accused’s version of events differs significantly from that of the alleged victim, adding that Haj himself sustained slight injuries according to a medical report.
The defence requested bail, arguing that the accused had no prior relationship with the victim and had no means of contacting him. They also stressed the need for CCTV footage to be reviewed and for other individuals involved in the incident to testify.
However, the court denied bail. The case is set to continue at a later date.
Following the incident, the Malta Ranger Unit called for increased police presence at Golden Bay, citing frequent fights and ongoing issues involving large groups gathering at the beach.
The magistrate presiding is Elaine Rizzo.
The prosecution is led by Inspector Warren Galea and Attorney General’s lawyer Joseph Camilleri Azarov.
The accused was defended by Thomas Barbara Sant.

4,000 Patients To Benefit From €6 Million Heart Medication Expansion
Health Minister Jo Etienne Abela announced a €6 million investment that will extend access to the medication Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan) through the Government formulary, benefiting 4,000 people.
The measure forms part of ongoing efforts to improve care for patients living with heart failure.
This chronic condition affects thousands of individuals and has a significant impact on quality of life. Recent medical advances have enabled more effective treatments that reduce the risk of hospitalisation and improve symptoms.
Entresto is a modern therapy already used successfully in several countries, particularly for patients who do not respond adequately to standard treatments.
The medication can be used by adults, adolescents and children aged one year and over who suffer from chronic heart failure. It is available in three different dosages to better match individual patient needs.
Abela said the initiative builds on other steps already taken, including the introduction of medications such as empagliflozin (Jardiance), and forms part of a broader vision that places patients at the centre of healthcare, with a focus on evidence-based treatment.
He explained that the investment reflects a commitment to modern and sustainable care. Implementation is expected to begin gradually in the coming days, with monitoring in place to ensure continuity of care.
The rollout will follow a structured process in line with the National Formulary, including clear eligibility criteria and prescribing protocols to ensure safe and equitable use. The procurement process is in its final stages.
“This measure helps safeguard patients with heart conditions and continues to strengthen a healthcare system that offers quality, equity and hope to all patients. The Government remains committed to providing the best possible care to patients in Malta and Gozo while safeguarding the sustainability of our systems,” Minister Abela said.

Partit Momentum Opposes Proposed ‘Monstrous’ Swieqi Overpass
Partit Momentum has voiced strong opposition to the proposed pedestrian overpass linking Swieqi and Pembroke, warning it could become a four-storey concrete structure imposed on residents without proper planning or consultation.
The party described the proposal as similar to the Msida pedestrian crossing project, claiming that despite expert advice, Minister Chris Bonett is proceeding with what it called another large-scale crossing in a central area.
“This project is another example of government incompetence. A major road was recently built, prioritising cars, yet no serious thought was given to pedestrian safety. Now, instead of fixing this failure properly, the only solution being presented is a massive overpass that will permanently scar the area,” said Momentum leader Arnold Cassola.
Momentum also expressed concern that alternative solutions were not properly explored. It said Infrastructure Malta and Transport Malta failed to consider a pedestrian underpass, which it described as a proven solution.
The party pointed to existing examples in Floriana near Bieb il-Bombi and Triq Sant’ Anna, as well as along the Birkirkara bypass connecting to Mater Dei, noting these were built decades ago and remain effective.
The party is proposing an underpass at the site, describing it as a safer, more practical, and less visually intrusive option.
“Following the Msida pedestrian crossing saga, unfortunately, this is yet another case of ignoring expert advice and highlights a serious failure in governance. Residents were not properly consulted, transparency is lacking, and key assessments, including visual impact and pedestrian usability, have not been presented,” said Cassola.
Momentum said it stands with residents of Swieqi and Pembroke who feel sidelined, and also criticised the Opposition and Nationalist Party for what it described as failing to represent concerns effectively.
People are being encouraged to attend a protest organised by the local council in Swieqi this Saturday at 10am.

New Space Secured For Birżebbuġa Band Club To Boost Fireworks Safety And Tradition
A field and an adjacent structure (defence post) in Triq il-Brolli, Birżebbuġa, currently under the responsibility of the Lands Authority, have been transferred to the San Pietru Philharmonic Society, Banda Birżebbuġa A.D. 1990, on a 15-year lease.
The main aim of this project is to provide enhanced facilities linked to the pyrotechnic activities carried out in the same area in connection with the annual village feast.
Given that pyrotechnic activity has already been taking place in this area for several years, the leased field – covering 350 square metres – will offer improved space for pyrotechnicians to launch aerial fireworks, thereby increasing safety for those involved.
The adjacent structure (defence post) will be restored and maintained by the society; however, the storage of pyrotechnic material within it will be prohibited.
The Minister for Culture, Lands and Local Government, Owen Bonnici, stated that this project is further confirmation of the Government’s confidence in Maltese festas.
“It is a clear commitment by the Government to continue strengthening and supporting societies in their work in favour of Maltese culture and the village feast, which is now also recognised as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The allocation of this new space is concrete proof of this commitment,” Dr Bonnici concluded.
The Chief Executive of the Lands Authority, Robert Vella, explained that this agreement reflects the Authority’s ongoing commitment to ensuring that public land is used sustainably and for the benefit of the community. He added that this project provides a more suitable and safer space for pyrotechnic activities, while also ensuring that existing structures are preserved and properly maintained.
“Through this transfer, the society now has peace of mind that it has an adequate space from which aerial fireworks can be launched. This will be done more safely, thanks to the increased space, and from this location the fireworks will be visible from the main square,” Mr Alex Caruana, President of the San Pietru Philharmonic Society, Banda Birżebbuġa A.D. 1990, said.
The transfer agreement was signed by the Chief Executive of the Lands Authority, Robert Vella; Alex Caruana, President of the San Pietru Philharmonic Society, Banda Birżebbuġa A.D. 1990; and Treasurer Walter Pace.

Court Orders Widow To Repay €15K Notary Deposit
A court has found Rosanne Barbara Zarb, widow of notary Ivan Barbara, liable for part of an unpaid client deposit handled by her late husband.
According to a report by Times of Malta, the court ordered Barbara Zarb and the notary’s estate to repay €15,000 to a client who had paid the sum as a deposit for a property contract.
The ruling requires Barbara Zarb to pay €7,500, with the remaining amount to be covered by the estate.
Barbara died in 2021 after contracting COVID-19 while abroad. Following his death, multiple clients filed claims to recover funds linked to property transactions.
Through her lawyers, Barbara Zarb argued she was not responsible for the debt, having renounced her husband’s inheritance.
She also cited evidence of his gambling habits in an attempt to show the funds were misappropriated.
However, the court, presided by Magistrate Victor G. Axiak, ruled that under Maltese law, professional debts can burden joint marital assets.
The case is one of several claims filed by clients seeking to recover deposits paid to the late notary.

Swieqi Deputy Mayor Jordan Galea Pace has raised concerns over worsening traffic in St Julian’s, Swieqi and surrounding areas, warning that congestion is no longer occasional but a daily reality.
“The situation is no longer a one off. It is becoming part of our daily life,” he said, highlighting growing frustration among residents and commuters.
Galea Pace pointed to a number of stalled or unclear infrastructure projects, questioning when the long-discussed Swieqi junction upgrade will move forward, noting that it appears to have been shelved.
He also raised concerns over the lack of a clear traffic management plan for new high-rise developments in St Julian’s that are nearing completion, as well as delays in opening the St Andrew’s hairpin near the Red Cross, which is expected to help ease congestion in the area.
The deputy mayor also referenced differing political positions on mass transport, noting that Prime Minister Robert Abela has said discussions will begin after the next general election, while Opposition MP Alex Borg has pledged to introduce a system within the first legislature.
“We cannot keep waiting, stuck in traffic and frustrated,” Galea Pace said. “We need real solutions. We need real change.”

Major Upgrade Planned For Wied Iż-Żurrieq With New Facilities And Safer Access
A major project aimed at upgrading and modernising facilities at Wied iż-Żurrieq has been approved, with works set to begin after the summer season.
The initiative, led by the Public Works Department, targets one of Malta’s most popular coastal locations, known for its views and access to the Blue Grotto. The investment is intended to improve the overall visitor experience while enhancing safety and accessibility.
The project includes upgrades to the ticket kiosk and facilities used by Blue Grotto boat operators, with a focus on improving efficiency and accessibility, including for persons with disabilities. Dedicated areas will also be introduced for divers to prepare safely before entering the water.
New storage facilities will be constructed for boatmen and fishermen to better protect boats and equipment, particularly during adverse weather conditions.
A central component of the project is the redesign of the jetty, which will provide safer and more accessible entry points for divers and swimmers, as well as improved mooring spaces for boats. Modern public sanitary facilities will also be installed.
The works will be carried out in line with environmental regulations, with measures in place to minimise impact on the surrounding ecosystem.
Parliamentary Secretary for Public Works Omar Farrugia said the project aims to improve conditions for workers who rely on the area, while also offering a better experience for visitors.
The project is expected to commence after the summer tourist season and be completed by the following summer.

AFM Soldiers Undergo Combat Marksmanship Training
The Armed Forces of Malta has begun a new round of combat marksmanship training aimed at strengthening soldiers’ operational readiness.
The programme, led by the First Regiment, focuses on improving shooting accuracy, reaction times and overall weapon handling in realistic combat scenarios.
Training includes engaging targets using cover, responding under pressure, and working with both service rifles and pistols. Soldiers are also being tested on their ability to handle moving targets and react quickly in dynamic situations.
The initiative forms part of ongoing efforts to ensure personnel remain prepared for a range of operational challenges, while maintaining discipline and high performance standards.

Rising Fuel Costs Could Impact Maltese Prices Next
Malta’s logistics sector is facing growing pressure as fuel prices surge across Europe, with operators warning of potential disruption to the country’s supply chain.
According to the Malta Independent, The Association of Maltese International Trailer Operators (ATTO) said diesel prices in Italy have exceeded €2 per litre, significantly increasing operating costs for haulage companies.
Fuel typically makes up 30–40% of costs, and recent increases of up to 30% are squeezing margins. ATTO Chairman Joseph Bugeja said early signs of strain are emerging, including limited fuel availability in some areas.
Operators are now adjusting routes and refuelling strategies to avoid disruption, with rising volatility affecting planning and delivery reliability.
Malta remains particularly exposed due to its reliance on European transport routes, with around 55,000 trailer movements annually supporting imports.
ATTO warned that continued cost increases could start impacting Malta more directly, potentially affecting supply chains and prices.
Should more be done to protect the sector?
